section.title: Pullbacks
section.p: ended with a practical problem. A form may be written in x,y,z-space, but the actual calculation may happen in a parameter domain. A curve uses a parameter t. A surface uses parameters u,v. A change of coordinates may use r,\theta,z or \rho,\phi,\theta.
So we need one operation that answers this question:
section.md: \text{How does a form look after we substitute the parametrization?}
section.p: That operation is called the pullback. The name sounds abstract, but the idea is ordinary substitution, done carefully enough to remember dx, dy, dz, and wedge products.

subsection.title: Substitution as the pullback idea
subsection.p: Start with a change of coordinates in the plane:
A point in the (u,v)-plane is sent to a point in the (x,y)-plane.
in the (x,y)-plane. We want to write this form in the (u,v)-coordinates.
means pull \omega back through T. The original form lived in the (x,y)-world. The pulled-back form lives in the (u,v)-world.

statement.p: be a smooth map into the plane. To pull back a form from the (x,y)-plane to the (u,v)-plane, use the substitutions
The resulting form is called the pullback and is written

subsection.p: A function is a 0-form, so its pullback is just ordinary composition:
For 1-forms, the pullback remembers the differentials. For 2-forms, it also remembers the wedge product.

statement.p: Pull back the basic area form:

md.mrow: \amp= du\wedge 2\,du - du\wedge dv + dv\wedge 2\,du - dv\wedge dv.

statement.p: The map multiplies signed area by -3. The negative sign says it reverses orientation.

subsection.p: This example is the pullback version of the Jacobian determinant. We will return to that connection at the end of the section.
The pullback obeys the algebra of forms:
These rules are not extra tricks. They say that after substitution, we still add, multiply by functions, and wedge exactly as before.

subsection.title: Pulling back 1-forms to curves
subsection.p: A curve is a map from a parameter interval into space:
Pulling a 1-form back to the curve turns it into a 1-form in the single variable t. That means it becomes something times dt.
subsection.md: \boxed{\mathbf r^*\omega = \bigl[ P(\mathbf r(t))x'(t) + Q(\mathbf r(t))y'(t) + R(\mathbf r(t))z'(t) \bigr]\,dt.}
subsection.p: This is exactly the line-integral integrand from earlier chapters. The pullback is the operation that produces it.

example.title: A form pulled back to a parabola

statement.p: The form is now ready to be integrated over the parameter interval.

example.title: A helix measuring rotation and height

md.mrow: \mathbf r^*\omega \amp= -\sin t(-\sin t\,dt) + \cos t(\cos t\,dt) + dt

statement.p: The rotational part contributes 1\,dt, and the vertical rise contributes another 1\,dt. Along this helix,

subsection.p: The curve supplies the tangent direction. The pullback asks the form to measure that direction and then rewrites the answer in the parameter variable.

subsection.title: Pulling back 2-forms to surfaces
subsection.p: A surface patch is described by two parameters:
A 2-form in space has the shape
Pulling it back to the (u,v)-plane gives something times
subsection.md: \boxed{\begin{aligned} \mathbf r^*\Phi \amp= \Bigl[ A(\mathbf r)(y_u z_v-y_v z_u) + B(\mathbf r)(z_u x_v-z_v x_u)\\ \amp\qquad\qquad + C(\mathbf r)(x_u y_v-x_v y_u) \Bigr]\,du\wedge dv. \end{aligned}}
subsection.p: The three coefficients are the three components of the cross product
subsection.md: \boxed{\mathbf r^*\Phi_{\mathbf F} = \mathbf F(\mathbf r(u,v))\cdot (\mathbf r_u\times\mathbf r_v)\,du\wedge dv.}
subsection.p: This is the classical flux formula, written as a pullback.

statement.p: Let the surface be the tilted plane patch
The original form measured oriented surface pieces in space. The pulled-back form measures the corresponding parameter pieces in the (u,v)-plane.

subsection.p: The pullback is not just a computational convenience. It is what makes it possible to integrate a form over a curved surface by integrating an ordinary form over a flat parameter region.

subsection.title: Pulling back volume forms under coordinate changes
subsection.p: For a coordinate change in space,
the basic volume form pulls back to
and similarly for dy and dz, the wedge product gives
The coefficient is the Jacobian determinant:
subsection.md: \boxed{\frac{\partial(x,y,z)}{\partial(u,v,w)} = \det \begin{pmatrix} x_u \amp x_v \amp x_w\\ y_u \amp y_v \amp y_w\\ z_u \amp z_v \amp z_w \end{pmatrix}.}

statement.p: This is the cylindrical volume factor from , but now the wedge product has also kept the orientation.

subsection.md: dx\wedge dy\wedge dz = r\,dr\wedge d\theta\wedge dz.
subsection.p: The factor is the same. The wedge notation remembers the sign.

subsection.title: The Jacobian determinant from wedge products
subsection.p: The Jacobian determinant is not a mysterious correction factor. It is the coefficient of a pulled-back volume form.
The absolute value of the Jacobian appears in ordinary area and volume computations because ordinary area and volume are unsigned:
Forms do not automatically take absolute values. They measure orientation.

statement.p: The map reflects the parameter square across a horizontal line. It preserves ordinary area but reverses orientation.
If we were computing unsigned area, the scale factor would be 1. But for an oriented 2-form, the scale factor is -1.

subsection.p: A pullback therefore does two jobs at once. It performs substitution, and it keeps track of how oriented length, area, or volume changes. Once a form has been pulled back to a parameter domain, the only remaining step is to integrate it.
